column1: You want to reproduce the SHA-1 hash Git produces for a file containing 'Hello, git\n' (11 bytes). What exact command?
column2: printf 'blob 11\0Hello, git\n' | shasum — Git prepends the header (type SP length NUL) before hashing. shasum on raw content alone gives a different result.

column1: You run cat on a file in .git/objects/ and get binary garbage. Why, and how do you read it correctly?
column2: Git stores all objects as zlib-compressed binary (DEFLATE algorithm). cat cannot decompress it. Use git cat-file -p <hash> to read any object, or Python's zlib.decompress() to see raw bytes. Always open the file in binary mode ('rb').

column1: What is a loose object in Git?
column2: An individual zlib-compressed object file stored at .git/objects/<first2>/<remaining38>. Every new object starts as a loose file. Packed into pack files by git gc or during push/clone operations.

column1: What is a pack file and what two files make up a pack?
column2: A pack file combines many objects into one using delta compression — solving the storage problem of thousands of loose files. Two files: pack-<hash>.pack (the objects) and pack-<hash>.idx (the index for fast lookup).

column1: What is delta compression in a Git pack file?
column2: One full 'base' object is stored. Similar objects are stored as deltas (diffs against the base). git verify-pack -v shows delta depth: 0 = full copy, N = chain of N diffs. Significantly reduces storage for incremental file changes.

Verbose human-readable storage statistics: count (loose objects), size (loose size), in-pack (objects in pack files), packs (pack file count), size-pack (total pack size), prune-packable (loose objects already packed).

column1: What triggers automatic garbage collection in Git?
column2: git gc --auto runs in the background when loose object count exceeds gc.auto threshold (default: ~6,700). Also run manually with git gc. Push/clone exchange pack files but do NOT trigger local gc.

column1: An unreachable object exists in .git/objects/. What determines when git gc removes it?
column2: Two config values: gc.pruneExpire (default: 2 weeks) — minimum age before a loose unreachable object is pruned. gc.reflogExpireUnreachable (default: 30 days) — how long unreachable reflog entries are protected. Until both windows expire, the object is recoverable via git cat-file -p <hash>.

column1: What command lists all objects in a pack file with their type, size, and delta depth?
column2: git verify-pack -v <path-to-.idx-file> — argument is the .idx file, NOT the .pack file. Delta depth 0 = stored as full object. Higher = delta chain depth.

Lists all objects in the database not reachable from any branch, tag, or ref — shown as 'dangling blob/commit/tree <hash>'. These are candidates for eventual removal by git gc. Can also be used to find orphaned blobs from git hash-object -w that were never committed.

column1: You open a Git object file with Python but get a zlib error. What is the most likely cause?
column2: The file was opened in text mode ('r') instead of binary mode ('rb'). zlib.decompress() requires raw bytes. Always use open(path, 'rb') for Git object files.

column1: Why does zlib compression work particularly well for Git's use case?
column2: Source code is text — highly repetitive, compresses 60-70% under DEFLATE. Decompression is fast (CPU cost negligible vs I/O savings). Same content always compresses identically at the same level, preserving the SHA-1 hash relationship.
